entries.title: Awwwards Element Decomposition + Three.js Progressive GLTF LOD
Joby Aviation Brand x WebGL Narrative & Codrops SVG Mask Scroll Transitions
entries.summary: Awwwards SOTD Mar 17: Joby Aviation wins Developer Award with a future-ready brand site built around the emotional legacy of flight — large-scale atmospheric WebGL environment, cinematic scroll pacing, and restrained typographic language that lets 3D scenery carry the brand story. Codrops Mar 11: Hiroki Watanabe's SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll demonstrates four distinct scroll-driven reveal patterns using SVG clipPath + GSAP ScrollTrigger, covering grid-based and venetian-blind-based mask geometries — no canvas, no WebGL.

blocks.text:
**Codrops (Mar 11, 2026) — SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll with GSAP and ScrollTrigger** by Hiroki Watanabe The tutorial builds four distinct scroll-triggered image reveal patterns using SVG `` as the masking layer, animated via GSAP ScrollTrigger. No WebGL, no canvas — pure DOM + SVG, accessible and performant. Four patterns: 1. **Grid Reveal:** image is masked by a grid of rectangles (`` elements inside ``). On scroll, each rect scales from 0→1 height in a staggered sequence — the image 'wipes in' through a tile grid. GSAP's `stagger` distributes the timeline across all rects. 2. **Venetian Blind:** horizontal strips (rects with full width, fixed height = 1/N of image), each revealing top-to-bottom at a slight offset. Same stagger pattern but strips feel like window blinds opening. 3. **Diagonal Wipe:** a single `` or `` inside clipPath, morphed from a flat line to full coverage using `MorphSVGPlugin` or manual path point lerping. 4. **Radial Burst:** circular/star `` that expands from center on scroll — `r` attribute of `` animated from 0 to max radius. Core code pattern (Grid Reveal): ```html
``` ```js import { gsap } from 'gsap'; import { ScrollTrigger } from 'gsap/ScrollTrigger'; gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger); const cells = document.querySelectorAll('.cell'); gsap.fromTo(cells, { scaleY: 0 }, { scaleY: 1, stagger: { amount: 0.6, from: 'random' }, ease: 'power3.out', scrollTrigger: { trigger: '.img-wrap', start: 'top 80%', end: 'top 20%', scrub: 1, } } ); ``` **Key insight:** `clipPathUnits="objectBoundingBox"` is the unlock — coordinates become 0–1 fractions of the element's bounding box, so the same SVG clipPath definition works regardless of image dimensions or viewport size. No pixel math, fully responsive. **Application for Ben:** The project case study hero image can use the venetian-blind reveal on scroll — strips open as user scrolls into the section. Amber-tinted strips (using a colored rect overlay in the same SVG layer, fading out as the reveal completes) would give the branded transition feel without any shader work.

links.label: Codrops: SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll (Hiroki Watanabe, Mar 11 2026)

entries.title: Three.js TSL Softbody Physics in the Browser & Codrops Clip-Path Morphing Text
entries.summary: Three.js community showcase: Niklas Niehus's Softbody Tetris runs real-time elastic deformation entirely on WebGPU via TSL (Three.js Shading Language) — GPU compute replaces physics CPU loops, opening deformable-mesh interactions for production web. Codrops Articles (Mar 12, 2026) by Huy: morphing text effect using SVG feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap filters, achieving liquid letter transitions without WebGL or canvas.

blocks.text:
**Codrops Articles (Mar 12, 2026) — SVG Filter Morphing Text** by Huy The technique: SVG filter primitives (`feTurbulence` + `feDisplacementMap`) applied via CSS `filter: url(#id)` to DOM text — creating liquid, melting letter transitions with zero WebGL, zero canvas, pure CSS + SVG. Core technique breakdown: - **`feTurbulence`** generates Perlin/fractal noise as a displacement map source. Key attributes: `baseFrequency` (scale of the distortion, ~0.02 for large waves), `numOctaves` (detail level, 2–4 is enough), `type='turbulence'` vs `type='fractalNoise'`. - **`feDisplacementMap`** reads the noise texture and displaces pixel positions by a `scale` factor — higher scale = more distortion. The `xChannelSelector='R' yChannelSelector='G'` tells it to use Red/Green channels for X/Y displacement. - **Animating with CSS `@keyframes`** on the SVG filter's `baseFrequency` or on the `feDisplacementMap scale` (via CSS custom property + `attr()`) — but since SVG filter attributes aren't directly animatable via CSS, the trick is: GSAP animates the `scale` attribute on the element directly, or a small JS snippet reads a CSS var and sets the attribute each frame. - **Morphing text:** 1. Two text layers overlaid 2. Layer A fades out while feTurbulence scale increases (letter dissolves into noise) 3. Layer B fades in while scale decreases (letter coalesces from noise) The illusion: text *melts* into and out of static ```html

DESIGN

``` ```js // Animate the displacement scale const displace = document.getElementById('displace'); gsap.to({ scale: 0 }, { scale: 80, duration: 0.6, ease: 'power2.in', onUpdate() { displace.setAttribute('scale', this.targets()[0].scale); } }); ``` **Key insight:** SVG filter effects on DOM elements are criminally underused. `feTurbulence` + `feDisplacementMap` gives you a visual language (organic deformation, dissolution, reformation) that looks like WebGL but requires nothing but a few lines of SVG and JS. Safari 17+, Chrome, Firefox all support it well. **Application for Ben:** Hero name/tagline morphing on page load — letters dissolve in from noise over 0.8s. Or the amber cursor leaves a brief displacement trail on text elements as it passes over them. Both achievable with this SVG filter approach, no Three.js required.

entries.title: 2-Color Video Portfolio & Three.js r183 NodeMaterial
entries.summary: Awwwards SOTD Mar 13: Adam Bricker's Film/TV/Animation portfolio wins Developer Award with a radical 2-color constraint (#F0F2F1 off-white + #020202 near-black), full-bleed video as the only visual currency. Three.js r183 ships NodeMaterial stable — GPU-agnostic node-based shader graphs that compile to both WebGL and WebGPU, replacing raw GLSL for most creative use cases.

blocks.text:
**Three.js r183 — NodeMaterial Stable & WebGPU Parity**

Three.js is now at r183 and the big story is **NodeMaterial reaching stable API** — a GPU-agnostic node-based shader system that compiles to GLSL (WebGL) *or* WGSL (WebGPU) from the same graph. This is the end of writing two shader versions.

Core concepts:
- **`Nodes` system:** replace raw `vertexShader`/`fragmentShader` strings with composable node graphs. `positionNode`, `colorNode`, `emissiveNode` are the entry points.
- **`MeshStandardNodeMaterial`:** drop-in for `MeshStandardMaterial` but accepts node inputs — mix procedural noise into a PBR material without touching GLSL.
- **`texture()` node + `uv()` node:** chain them for animated UV distortion: `texture(map, uv().add(timerLocal().mul(0.1)))` scrolls a texture over time.
- **WebGPU backend:** `WebGPURenderer` is now production-ready. Falls back to WebGL if WebGPU unavailable (one `await renderer.init()` call handles detection).

```js
import { MeshStandardNodeMaterial, texture, uv, timerLocal, vec2 } from 'three/nodes';

const material = new MeshStandardNodeMaterial();
const t = timerLocal(); // auto-updates each frame

// Animated UV offset: texture scrolls diagonally
material.colorNode = texture(
  myTexture,
  uv().add(vec2(t.mul(0.05), t.mul(0.03)))
);
```

**Why this matters:** NodeMaterial eliminates the biggest friction in creative Three.js work — shader strings that break on GLSL version changes and can't be easily composed. The node graph approach is how Blender's shader editor works; if you can think in nodes, you can now build the same effects in-browser.

**Application for Ben:** A subtle animated texture on the hero section background (slow UV scroll of a noise texture, amber-tinted) — previously required writing GLSL. With NodeMaterial: ~5 lines of JavaScript.

entries.title: Swiss Grid × WebGL Geometry & Typography-Led Corporate Motion
entries.summary: Codrops Mar 5: Corentin Bernadou's portfolio uses Swiss editorial grid rules to govern Three.js geometry placement — structure-first, then WebGL. Awwwards SOTD Mar 11: Outsource Consultants wins Developer Award with restrained, typography-led layout that proves motion discipline beats effects excess.

blocks.text:
**Codrops (Mar 5, 2026) — Swiss Grid Meets WebGL Geometry**

Corentin Bernadou's portfolio redesign is an architecture lesson: start with a strict Swiss editorial grid (baseline grid, column lock, clear hierarchy) and *then* layer WebGL geometry into the empty white space — not as decoration, but as geometric punctuation.

Key techniques:
- **Grid-constrained WebGL:** 3D geometry (spheres, toruses) are positioned at grid intersections, not freestyle. This stops WebGL from feeling chaotic.
- **Thoughtful motion timing:** transitions use `cubic-bezier(0.76, 0, 0.24, 1)` — the S-curve with a slow-out, which feels editorial rather than playful.
- **Geometry as accent:** same role as a bold pull-quote in print layout. The 3D element earns its space by framing text, not competing with it.

**Application for Ben:** The Works page grid could have a single Three.js plane positioned at the column gutter — not image distortion, just a subtle mesh that reacts to scroll. Structural, not decorative.

blocks.label: Swiss Grid Punctuation
blocks.description: Editorial grid with a WebGL-style geometric accent element. Structure first — geometry earns its place at grid intersections.

entries.title: Scroll-Velocity WebGL Gallery & ON Energy Glass UI
entries.summary: Today's picks: Codrops scroll-driven WebGL gallery (scroll velocity drives shader distortion), and Awwwards SOTD ON Energy's ultra-thin glass panel language.

blocks.text:
**Codrops (Mar 9, 2026) — Scroll-Velocity WebGL Gallery**

Key technique: pipe scroll velocity as a uniform into Three.js shader. Higher velocity = more distortion, release = snap back. Unlike static hover distortion, this makes *motion itself* the visual content.

- `previousScrollY` diff → clamp to -1~1 velocity
- Vertex shader: `sin(uv.y + time) * velocity * strength`
- Background color shifts with image palette (palette-driven immersion)

**Application:** Ben's Works grid could use this — fast scroll makes images "flow", slow/stop = they settle back.

entries.title: WebGL Hover Distortion & Grain Texture
entries.summary: Deep dive into 2025-2026 top creative sites. Focus: WebGL shader hover distortion and noise grain texture overlays.

blocks.text: **stabondar.com** — project grid looks like CSS grid, but each image has a Three.js mesh layered on top. On hover, mouse velocity is injected as a uniform → vertex shader computes ripple distortion. Effect: images flow like liquid under the cursor.

blocks.label: Grain Texture Overlay
blocks.description: CSS + SVG feTurbulence gives film grain feel. Near-zero cost, significant texture upgrade.
blocks.css: body{margin:0;background:#F7F5F0;font-family:'EB Garamond',serif;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;height:100vh;overflow:hidden}.grain-demo{position:relative;padding:40px;text-align:center}.grain-demo p{font-size:32px;margin:0 0 16px;color:#1a1a1a}.tag{font-size:11px;letter-spacing:2px;color:#C17A3A;font-family:monospace}.grain-demo::after{content:'';position:fixed;inset:-50%;width:200%;height:200%;background-image:url("data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%3E%3Cfilter id='n'%3E%3CfeTurbulence type='fractalNoise' baseFrequency='0.9' numOctaves='4' stitchTiles='stitch'/%3E%3C/filter%3E%3Crect width='100%25' height='100%25' filter='url(%23n)' opacity='1'/%3E%3C/svg%3E");opacity:0.045;pointer-events:none;animation:grain 0.5s steps(2) infinite}@keyframes grain{0%,100%{transform:translate(0,0)}25%{transform:translate(-2px,1px)}50%{transform:translate(1px,-1px)}75%{transform:translate(-1px,2px)}}

blocks.label: Magnetic Cursor
blocks.description: Mouse snaps toward element on proximity, releases on exit. Good for CTA buttons and tags.
blocks.js: const btn=document.getElementById('btn');let rect,active=false;btn.addEventListener('mouseenter',()=>{rect=btn.getBoundingClientRect();active=true});btn.addEventListener('mouseleave',()=>{active=false;btn.style.transform='translate(0,0)'});document.addEventListener('mousemove',e=>{if(!active)return;const x=e.clientX-rect.left-rect.width/2;const y=e.clientY-rect.top-rect.height/2;const dist=Math.sqrt(x*x+y*y);const maxDist=80;if(dist<maxDist){const force=(maxDist-dist)/maxDist;btn.style.transform=`translate(${x*force*0.4}px,${y*force*0.4}px)`;rect=btn.getBoundingClientRect()}});

blocks.text: **2026 visual language trends:** Minimal base + single high-contrast accent (Ben's amber is spot on). Ultra Light + Bold weight contrast. Anti-UI: no card borders, whitespace as separator. Noise grain adds analog warmth.
